text: Training intensities of 40-50% VO2max can increase VO2max substantially in untrained individuals but are insufficient for well-trained distance runners.
The minimum training intensity needed to enhance VO2max is highly dependent on the runner's initial VO2max level, with fitter runners requiring higher intensities.
Training at or near 95-100% VO2max may be necessary for well-trained distance runners to elicit further VO2max improvements, as submaximal adaptations have likely already been achieved.

Physiological adaptations from training at VO2max intensity include increased maximal stroke volume, skeletal muscle capillarisation, myoglobin concentration, and oxidative capacity of type II muscle fibres.
The strength of cardiovascular and muscular adaptation stimuli is exercise-intensity dependent up to VO2max, meaning higher intensities produce stronger adaptive signals.

Lower training intensities (e.g. 70-80% VO2max) may produce similar VO2max adaptations in moderately trained runners because the physiological stress can be sustained for longer durations, but this does not apply to well-trained runners.
Well-trained distance runners who appear to plateau in VO2max can break through that plateau by incorporating training protocols that elicit 95-100% VO2max.
